messages.content:
Which of the following characterise a classroom in which the enquiry approach is followed?
Select one:
a. The teacher has content knowledge
b. Learners engage in discussion between themselves.
c. All the options
d. The teacher asks the questions.
c. All the options

Select the correct statement
Select one:
a. Neither the relation 'is more than' nor is the relation 'is 3 more than' is transitive.
b. The relation 'is more than' is transitive but the relation 'is 3 more than' is not transitive.
c. Both the relation 'is more than' and the relation 'is 3 more than' are transitive.
d. None of the options
b. The relation 'is more than' is transitive but the relation 'is 3 more than' is not transitive.

Select the best teaching approach for learners who are likely to struggle when introduced to exponents.
Select one:
a. Ask them to memorise the laws of exponents.
b. All the options
c. Ask them to divide
d. Ask them to use expansion.
a. Ask them to memorise the laws of exponents.

What prior knowledge is needed for a good understanding of linear graphs?
Select one:
a. Algebra
b. ate.
c. Transformation geometry.
d. Data handling.
a. Algebra

Learners need a good understanding of fractions before they can tackle problems involving ratio and ......?
Select one:
a. All the options
b. Decimals
c. Whole numbers
d. Proportion
d. Proportion

Give the co-ordinates of (–1;–7) after reflection in the y-axis.
Select one:
a. (1;–7)
b. (- 1;-7)
c. (–1;7)
d. (1;7)

Select the correct statement
Select one:
a. For a function, every output has only one output
b. For a function, every input has only one input
c. For a function, every input has only one output.
d. For a function, every output has only one input.
c. For a function, every input has only one output.

Relation that is valid both forwards and in reverse.E.g.6 = 5 + 1 and 5 + 1 = 6
Select one:
a. Relation
b. Reversal error
c. Symmetric
d. Reciprocal
c. Symmetric

Mathematics is understood as.......
Select one:
a. Solving mathematical problems
b. Calculations
c. a language that makes use of symbols and notations to describe numerical, geometric and graphical relationships.
d. All the options
c. a language that makes use of symbols and notations to describe numerical, geometric and graphical relationships.

Funnelling involves
Select one:
a. Learner discussion
b. Teacher facilitation
c. Over-simplification of problem
d. Teacher asking clarifying questions
d. Teacher asking clarifying questions

What is the danger of teaching learners the rule 'take it over and change the sign'?
Select one:
a. It only refers to addition and subtraction, not to multiplication or division.
b. It only works for positive numbers
c. All the options
d. It is incorrect

c. All the options
This rule is a common method for remembering how to subtract a signed number, but it is limited in its application. It only applies to addition and subtraction, not to multiplication or division. Additionally, it only works for positive numbers, not negative numbers. Therefore, relying solely on this rule can lead to misconceptions and errors in understanding and applying signed number operations.

The two elementary mathematical operations of arithmetic which are extended into other fields of mathematics, such as algebra and measurement.
Select one:
a. Subtraction and division
b. Addition and multiplication
c. Addition and subtraction
d. Multiplication and division
b. Addition and multiplication

Set of points defined by pairs of co-ordinates which refer to numbers on the horizontal axis (the x-axis) and the vertical axis (the y-axis).
Select one:
a. Dependent variable
b. Cartesian plane
c. Coefficient
d. Function
b. Cartesian plane

The teacher prepares a diagram showing 8 rectangles, Which question should he ask when demonstrating division by one half?
Select one:
a. How many times does 8 fit into 8?
b. How many times does one half fit into 8?
c. How many times does 2 fit into 8?
d. How many triangles do we have?
b. How many times does one half fit into 8?

How many sixths of a chocolate bar must you put together to make a third of a chocolate bar?
Select one:
a. None of the options
b. 4
c. 2
d. 1

c. 2
To make a third of a chocolate bar using sixths, you would need to combine 2 sixths of a chocolate bar.

To work with fractions, algebraic texts and more complex problems, learners need to understand that,
Select one:
a. None of the options
b. for a x b = c, a is a factor of c
c. for a x b = c, b is a factor of c
d. for a x b = c, a and b are both factors of c

d. for a x b = c, a and b are both factors of c
In the equation a x b = c, both a and b are factors of c.

Give the co-ordinates of (–1;–7) after reflection in the line y=x.
Select one:
a. (–7;–1)
b. (–1;7)
c. (7;1)
d. (1;7)

Which of the following formulae should a Grade 9 learner be able to use?
Select one:
a. Compound interest
b. Speed, distance, time
c. Simple interest, speed, distance, and time
d. Simple interest

c. Simple interest, speed, distance, and time
A Grade 9 learner should be able to use the formula for simple interest, as well as the formulas relating to speed, distance, and time.

Which of the following formulae should a Grade 9 learner be able to use?
Select one:
a. Compound interest
b. Speed, distance, time
c. Simple interest, speed, distance, and time
d. Simple interest
d. Simple interest

Describe a way of folding a sheet of paper that can be used to demonstrate 1/4 of 1/3=1/12
Select one:
a. None of the options
b. First fold the paper into five parts then into three parts
c. First fold the paper into four parts then into three parts.
d. First fold the paper into three parts then into four parts.

b. First fold the paper into five parts then into three parts.

To demonstrate 1/4 of 1/3 = 1/12, you can fold the paper into five equal parts first. Then, fold one of the sections into three equal parts. This way, you will have a total of 15 smaller sections on the paper. If you select and count only one section out of the 15, you will find that it represents 1/12 of the entire sheet, which demonstrates the multiplication of fractions.

Learners apply what they already know to assist them during the transition from one year to the next, this is supported when teachers use?
Select one:
a. authentic problems
b. extended investigation.
c. open-ended questions
d. All the options

d. All the options

When teachers use authentic problems, extended investigation, and open-ended questions, they are supporting learners in applying what they already know during the transition from one year to the next. These approaches encourage critical thinking, problem-solving, and the application of prior knowledge in new and meaningful contexts.

Functions are an important part of
Select one:
a. Algebra
b. exponents
c. Mathematics
d. statistics
c. Mathematics
